text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 21 September 2025 

Dr A. Patel  
St Cuthbert NHS Neurology Centre  
Outpatients, 12 Wren Street, Newcastle upon Tyne NE1 5QT  

Dear Dr A. Patel,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1992, Hospital No: NHS8472031 NHS No. 6049182753  
Flat 3B, 27 Jesmond Vale, Newcastle upon Tyne, NE2 1QX 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification; prior MRI (2022) reported as structurally unremarkable; prior routine EEG (2023) non-diagnostic. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte 

Plan: 

- Continue current antiseizure regimen without change today  
- Safety counselling reinforced; SUDEP information discussed; nocturnal supervision and seizure alarm continue  
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFTs, Vitamin D  
- Consider add-on therapy or dose optimisation at next review pending seizure diary and tolerability  
- Follow-up in 4 months; earlier review if deterioration  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today with both parents present. They report that sleep is often fragmented, and he has recently been working irregular shifts. He drinks alcohol socially and is currently reducing his intake, having noticed a possible temporal association between heavier weekend use and poorer nights. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 to 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. He keeps a seizure diary, which shows clustering around periods of stress and sleep deprivation. There have been no recent hospital admissions, head injuries, or status epilepticus. 

He tolerates Levetiracetam without clear mood change, and uses Clobazam at night; morning sedation is mild and improving. No rashes, jaundice, or focal neurological complaints. Weight stable. On examination today he was alert and oriented, speech fluent, cranial nerves normal, power and tone symmetrical, gait steady. Cardiorespiratory and abdominal examination were unremarkable. 

We discussed trigger management in detail (sleep routine, hydration, alcohol moderation). He and his family prefer not to escalate therapy immediately, aiming first to consolidate lifestyle measures while continuing reduction of alcohol intake. I provided a written seizure action plan and updated his employer letter regarding reasonable adjustments for shift patterns. 

No changes were made to medication today. We will review again in four months with seizure diary and blood results; if the current pattern persists, we will consider lamotrigine add-on or clobazam titration. Driving remains not permitted under current DVLA guidance given ongoing events; this was re-iterated. 

With best wishes, 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 to 3 nights per week

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 to 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 to 3 nights per week". It also mentions "drop attacks occurring in batches" and "tonic seizures during both day and night," but provides no numeric frequency for those types. Per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple frequencies exist and are quantifiable, the only explicit numeric frequency is 2 to 3 per week for generalized tonic-clonic seizures. No other numeric frequency is provided to compare or sum. Therefore, the current seizure frequency is 2 to 3 per week.
seizure_frequency_number: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 to 3 nights per week
